ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a prepared Indo-Chinese chicken dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Indo-Chinese Chilli Chicken. A0CKN (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for Indo-Chinese Chilli Chicken as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Chilli Chicken is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a plate of batter-fried chicken pieces tossed in spicy Indo-Chinese sauce with bell peppers and onions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese Chilli Chicken exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken preparations and USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for cooked chicken with sauce components. Macros and micronutrients are consistent with a deep-fried, stir-fried chicken dish with soy-chili sauce. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indo-Chinese Chilli Chicken. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with battered and fried chicken dishes combined with cornstarch-thickened sauces and added sugars, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed chicken dishes with starch coatings. Per the validator's classification standard, GI 55 is categorised as 'low' (threshold: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 40) / 100 = 22.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per standard 300 g serving) from soy sauce and chili sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid Chilli Chicken due to its very high sodium load; if consumed, choose the dry version over gravy and restrict portion to no more than 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined cornstarch batter coating and added sugars in sauces contribute to a GI of 55 and a high glycemic load per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Chilli Chicken to small portions (100–150 g), prefer the dry version to reduce sugar-laden gravy, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds total fat and trans fat; high sodium exacerbates cardiovascular risk; cholesterol content is moderate at 50 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Chilli Chicken due to its combination of high sodium, deep-fried fat content, and trans fat; occasional consumption of a small portion (100 g) in the context of an otherwise heart-healthy diet may be acceptable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation and high-sodium sauces may promote systemic inflammation; nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, green chilies) may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their response to Chilli Chicken, particularly its nightshade content and deep-fried fat, and limit consumption during flare periods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Chicken is a quintessential Indo-Chinese dish that is enormously popular across India and among South Asian diaspora communities worldwide. It consists of bite-sized chicken pieces that are coated in a seasoned cornstarch-and-egg batter and deep-fried until crispy, then stir-fried in a wok with a bold, savory-spicy sauce made from soy sauce, chili sauce, vinegar, ginger, garlic, and green chilies. Chunky pieces of bell peppers and onions are added for texture and flavor. The dish is available in two main versions: a dry version served as an appetizer or snack, and a gravy version served as a main course alongside fried rice or noodles. The flavor profile is intensely savory, spicy, and slightly tangy with a hint of sweetness from the sauces. Nutritionally, Chilli Chicken provides approximately 217 kcal per 100 g, with 15 g protein, 11.7 g fat, and 13.3 g carbohydrate. It is notably high in sodium (~400 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The GI of 55 (classified as low by the validator standard) and moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving are relevant for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a good source of niacin (5.2 mg), vitamin B6 (0.35 mg), phosphorus (140 mg), and potassium (210 mg) from the chicken component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chilli Chicken?
Is Chilli Chicken good for diabetics?
Can I eat Chilli Chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese Chilli Chicken exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken preparations and USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken data, consistent with the described preparation method. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Indo-Chinese Chilli Chicken is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g of 0.07 g per 100 g is retained from input data, consistent with small residual trans fat from deep-frying oil; value is plausible and within expected range for deep-fried chicken. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (55 × 40 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 22.0. gi_category set to 'low' per validator classification standard where GI ≤55 = low, 56–69 = medium, ≥70 = high. The dietary_tag 'low_sugar' from the input has been removed as sugar content of 8.33 g per 100 g (25 g per 300 g serving) does not meet a low-sugar classification. Portions in the input contained multiple duplicates; a single canonical serving of 300 g (1 plate) has been used as the default. Halal status is assumed as typical for Indian restaurant preparation but should be verified for specific vendors.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, applied ICMR-NIN source, populated all mandatory sections.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'broader'/'adapted' to 'closest_parent'. Retained gi_category 'medium' for GI 55.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' per validator classification standard (GI ≤55 = low). Updated gi_source notes and llm_training description to reflect corrected category. Updated notes field to document the gi_category correction rationale.
